prompt 1: You are a software requirements engineer. You are tasked to create a python script that loads a cleaned dataset of reviews and automatically group the similar reviews using a clustering or grouping approach. Acceptable approaches include semantic similarity grouping, embedding clustering, or LLM-based grouping, or a combination of them, in any way that generates meaningful results. Use the Groq API with meta-llama/llama-4- scout-17b-16e-instruct model. Save the results in a file at data/review_groups_auto.json. Each group must contain at least several related reviews and clearly represent a common theme. I have attached the clean dataset for more context (its directory is data/reciews_clean.jsonl). You should have 5 groups, each with a minimum of 10 reviews. Make sure to add meaningful comments. This is the template to use for the group json file {   groups: [     {       group_id: A1,       theme: Workout logging issues,       review_ids: [rev_12, rev_33, rev_41, rev_92],       example_reviews: [         The app crashes whenever I try to log a run.,         Logging workouts causes the app to freeze.       ]     }   ] }
prompt 2: Step 2: Use the review groups you created in step 1 to automatically create personas. Add to the same python script and don't delete any of the original functionality. The script must now generate structured persona objects using an LLM prompt or automated summarization. Use the same model as before. Save the output in: personas/personas_auto.json. Each persona must reference the review group it was derived from. This is the template it should be saved in:  {  personas: [     {       id: P1,       name: Frequent Activity Tracker,       description: A user who relies on the app to log workouts frequently and expects stable performance.,       derived_from_group: G1,       goals: [         Track workouts consistently,         Log activity quickly      ],       pain_points: [         App crashes during logging,         Workout data sometimes disappears      ],       context: [         Logging activities immediately after workouts,         Using the app during outdoor exercise      ],       constraints: [         Logging must be reliable,         Data should not be lost       ],       evidence_reviews: [         rev_12,         rev_45      ]     }  ] }
prompt 3: Step 3: Create a new script. This script must read the automated personas you generated from the directory personas/personas_auto.json and generate structured system requirements. Save the generated specification in: spec/spec_auto.md. Each requirement must include: a unique requirement ID, a description of system behavior, the persona that motivated the requirement, traceability to the review group, and acceptance criteria. Write at least 10 requirements. The format must follow the following template: # Requirement ID: FR_auto_1 - Description: [The system shall allow users to log workouts without application crashes.] - Source Persona: [Active Fitness Tracker] - Traceability: [Derived from review group A1] - Acceptance Criteria:[Given the user logs a workout, When the logging process completes, Then the application must remain stable, and the workout must be saved successfully.]
prompt 4: Step 4: Generate Validation Tests Automatically: Create validation tests based on the automatically generated specifications you created in the last step. Each requirement must include at least one validation scenario that describes how it could be verified. Implement this by creating a new python script that reads the requirements from: spec/spec_auto.md and generate structured test scenarios.  Save the generated tests in: tests/tests_auto.json. Each test scenario must contain: a unique test ID, the requirement it validates, a short scenario description, a list of steps describing how the test would be executed, and the expected outcome. Each requirement in the specification must be linked to at least one test scenario. The script should ensure that every test references a requirement ID, that the steps are written clearly, and that the expected result reflects the requirement being validated. Use the following format: {   tests: [     {       test_id: T_auto_1,       requirement_id: FR_auto_1,       scenario: Logging a workout without crashing,       steps: [         Open the workout logging screen,         Enter workout details,         Submit the workout       ],       expected_result: The workout is saved successfully and the application remains stable.     }   ] }
prompt 5: Step 5: Create a new python script to compute Metrics for the Automated Pipeline: Save the results in: metrics/metrics_auto.json. It should be in the following format:  {   pipeline: automated,   dataset_size: 300,   persona_count: 4,   requirements_count: 8,   tests_count: 10,   traceability_links: 12,   review_coverage: 0.65,   traceability_ratio: 0.88,   testability_rate: 0.80,    ambiguity_ratio: 0.12 } These are the descriptions for each field: • Dataset size: The total number of cleaned user reviews available in data/reviews_clean.jsonl after preprocessing. • Persona count: The number of personas created from manually identified review groups in personas/personas_manual.json. • Requirements count: The total number of software requirements documented in the manual specification file spec/spec_manual.md. • Tests count: The number of validation test scenarios defined in tests/tests_manual.json. • Traceability links: The number of explicit traceable relationships between artifacts in the pipeline. • Review coverage ratio: The ratio (0-1) of reviews that have been covered by the personas generated. • Traceability ratio: The proportion of requirements that can be traced back to a persona through an explicit reference in the specification. • Testability rate: The proportion of requirements that have at least one associated validation test scenario in tests/tests_manual.json. • Ambiguity ratio: The proportion of requirements whose descriptions or acceptance criteria contain ambiguous or non-measurable language (e.g., vague terms such as fast, easy, better, user-friendly, etc.) that lead to multiple interpretations during testing.
prompt 6: Step6: In this task, update the metrics.py script you created to compute the metrics for the hybrid pipelines as well. Do not remove any of the original functionality. Save the output for the hybrid metrics in metrics/metrics_hybrid.json. In the following format:  {   pipeline: hybrid,   dataset_size: 300,   persona_count: 4,   requirements_count: 8,   tests_count: 9,   traceability_links: 12,   review_coverage: 0.67,   traceability_ratio: 0.88,   testability_rate: 0.87,    ambiguity_ratio: 0.12 } The hybrid pipeline has different id formats for each of the artifacts than the automatic pipeline. Here are the id formats you should use for the hybrid one.       group_id: H1,       theme: Payment and Subscription Issues,       review_ids: [         7c0ea9e5-b2a2-4128-b544-a09ad3d34bac,   personas: [     {       id: P_hybrid_1, # Requirement ID: FR_auto_1   tests: [     {       test_id: T_hybrid_1,       requirement_id: FR_hybrid_1,
prompt 7: Step 7: in this task implement a python script that runs all the scripts in the pipeline from start to finish. implement: src/run_all.py. This script must execute the automated workflow from start to finish. It should load the raw dataset, clean it, generate automated review groups and personas, create specifications, generate tests, and compute metrics. The script does not need to automate manual or hybrid thinking steps, but it must automate all steps intended to be generated programmatically. Add comments in run_all.py describing the order of execution and the files produced at each stage.  This is the order of execution: 1. run src/02_clean.py which writes to data/reviews_clean.jsonl 2. run src/05_personas_auto.py3. run src/06_spec_generate.py4. run src/07_test_generate.py5. run src/08_metrics.py I have attached these files for your reference
prompt 8: Last step: Implement a repository validation script: src/00_validate_repo.py This script must check whether all required folders and required files exist. It should print a clear message indicating whether the repository structure is complete: Checking repository structure... data/reviews_clean.jsonl found personas/personas_manual.json found spec/spec_auto.md found tests/tests_hybrid.json found Repository validation complete
